exercises.title: Trace basic countdown
exercises.scenario: Predict what a simple recursive method prints.

exercises.title: Complete the base case
exercises.instructions: Fill in the base case to prevent infinite recursion.

blanks.hint: What value of n stops recursion?

hints: When n reaches 0, return 1 (NOT 0 — would make everything 0)

exercises.title: Add missing base case
exercises.scenario: This code will cause StackOverflowError. Fix it.
exercises.instructions:
Add the base case so this prints 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 (each on own line) without crashing.

Expected output:
5
4
3
2
1

bugs.issue: No base case — recursion never stops, eventually causes StackOverflowError
bugs.fix: Add `if (n == 0) return;` at the start of the method

hints: Without a base case, this calls itself forever
Eventually Java runs out of stack space → StackOverflowError
Add `if (n == 0) return;` to stop when n reaches 0
Must be BEFORE the println and recursive call

exercises.title: Sum 1 to N recursively
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `RecursiveSum` with:
1. A method `sum(int n)` that returns 1 + 2 + ... + n using recursion
2. main calls sum(5) and prints result

Base case: sum(0) = 0
Recursive case: sum(n) = n + sum(n-1)

Expected output:
Sum 1 to 5: 15
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your RecursiveSum class below
// Must use recursion, NOT a loop

exercises.title: Count up recursively
exercises.scenario: Print numbers in ASCENDING order using recursion.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `CountUp` with a recursive method that prints 1 to 5 (ascending order):

Expected output:
1
2
3
4
5

Hint: place println AFTER the recursive call (so deeper calls return before printing).
hints: Trace: countUp(5) recurses to countUp(0) first, then prints 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 as stack unwinds
Print BEFORE recurse = descending. Print AFTER recurse = ascending.

exercises.title: Trace factorial
Power function
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Power` with a recursive method `power(int base, int exponent)` that calculates base^exponent.

Base case: anything^0 = 1
Recursive case: base^n = base * base^(n-1)

main tests with 2^10:

Expected output:
2^10 = 1024
hints: Base case: when exponent reaches 0, return 1
Notice: base stays the same, exponent decreases

exercises.title: Recursive Fibonacci
exercises.scenario: Implement Fibonacci using recursion (the classic example).
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Fib` with a recursive method `fib(int n)` that returns the nth Fibonacci number.

Base cases:
- fib(0) = 0
- fib(1) = 1

Recursive case: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

main prints fib(0) through fib(10):

Expected output:
fib(0) = 0
fib(1) = 1
fib(2) = 1
fib(3) = 2
fib(4) = 3
fib(5) = 5
fib(6) = 8
fib(7) = 13
fib(8) = 21
fib(9) = 34
fib(10) = 55
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your Fib class below
// Use recursion (we'll explore the slowness later)
hints: TWO base cases: n == 0 returns 0, n == 1 returns 1
main uses a loop to print 11 values (0 to 10)
Note: this is slow for n > 30 due to repeated computations — fine for demonstrating recursion

exercises.title: Fix recursive case that doesn't progress
exercises.scenario: This code causes StackOverflowError despite having a base case. Find why.
exercises.instructions:
The code below has a base case but still crashes. The recursive case doesn't move toward the base case. Fix it.

Expected output:
10

bugs.issue: Recursive call uses (n + 1), moving AWAY from base case (0). Will recurse forever.
bugs.fix: Change to (n - 1) to move TOWARD the base case

hints: If we call decreaseUntilZero(n + 1), n keeps growing
It never reaches 0 — infinite recursion → crash
Change to decreaseUntilZero(n - 1) so it decreases each call

exercises.title: Sum of digits (recursive)
exercises.scenario: Compute sum of digits recursively (compare to Topic 1.5 iterative version).
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `DigitSum` with recursive method `sumOfDigits(int n)` that returns the sum of n's digits.

Base case: if n < 10, return n
Recursive case: return (n % 10) + sumOfDigits(n / 10)

main demonstrates with 12345:

Expected output:
Sum of digits of 12345: 15
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your DigitSum class below
// Must use recursion, NOT a loop
hints: Base case: single digit (n < 10) — return n itself
Recursive: last digit (n % 10) + sum of rest (sumOfDigits(n / 10))
Compare: iterative version had 8 lines, recursive has 4

exercises.title: Trace recursive pattern
exercises.scenario: Predict output when print is positioned differently.
exercises.expected_solution:
Before: 3
Before: 2
Before: 1
After: 1
After: 2
After: 3

test_cases.expected:
Before: 3
Before: 2
Before: 1
After: 1
After: 2
After: 3

hints: All 'Before's print on the way DOWN; all 'After's print on the way UP

exercises.title: Count occurrences in number
exercises.scenario: Count how many times a specific digit appears in a number.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `DigitCount` with recursive method `countDigit(int number, int target)` that returns how many times `target` digit appears in `number`.

Examples:
- countDigit(12321, 2) = 2
- countDigit(55555, 5) = 5
- countDigit(12345, 9) = 0

Base case: if number == 0, return 0
Recursive case: 1 if last digit matches target, plus result for rest of digits

Expected output:
Count of 2 in 12321: 2
Count of 5 in 55555: 5
Count of 9 in 12345: 0

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Count of 2 in 12321: 2
Count of 5 in 55555: 5
Count of 9 in 12345: 0

hints: Base case: number == 0, return 0 (no more digits)
Recursive call on rest: countDigit(number / 10, target)
If last digit matches target, add 1 to the recursive result
Else just return the recursive result

exercises.title: Recursive reverse number
exercises.scenario: Build a number by extracting and reassembling digits — needs creative recursion.
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your ReverseNumber class below
// Use a helper method with an accumulator
hints: Use TWO methods: reverse (public wrapper) and reverseHelper (does the work)
Helper takes an accumulator parameter starting at 0
Each call: shift reversed left (* 10), add new last digit (n % 10)
Trace reverse(123): helper(123, 0) → helper(12, 3) → helper(1, 32) → helper(0, 321) → returns 321

exercises.title: Recursive vs iterative comparison
exercises.scenario: Implement the SAME problem two ways and compare.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Comparison` with TWO methods computing 1+2+3+...+n:

1. `sumIterative(int n)` — uses a for loop
2. `sumRecursive(int n)` — uses recursion

main compares results for n = 10:

Expected output:
Iterative sum(10): 55
Recursive sum(10): 55
Match: true
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your Comparison class below
// Implement BOTH iterative and recursive versions

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Iterative sum(10): 55
Recursive sum(10): 55
Match: true

hints: Iterative: standard accumulator pattern with for loop
Recursive: base case n==0 returns 0, recursive case n + sumRecursive(n-1)

exercises.title: Greatest Common Divisor (Euclidean algorithm)
exercises.scenario: Implement Euclid's algorithm — one of the oldest algorithms (2000+ years old), still used in cryptography today.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `GCD` with a recursive method `gcd(int a, int b)` using Euclid's algorithm:

- If b == 0, gcd(a, b) = a (base case)
- Otherwise, gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a % b)

This efficiently finds the GCD — converges in O(log min(a,b)) steps.

main tests with several pairs:
```
gcd(48, 36) = 12
gcd(100, 75) = 25
gcd(17, 5) = 1
gcd(1071, 462) = 21
```
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your GCD class below
// Euclid's algorithm: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a % b) until b == 0
hints: Base case: when b reaches 0, gcd is a
Recursive: swap them, with second becoming a % b
Beautiful algorithm — proven to terminate, very fast
Used in RSA encryption, fraction simplification, scheduling problems
